Access Control Assistant (ACA) 
The Access Control Assistant (ACA) is a user interface that allows administrators to define and manage access 
control of the AVEVA base product model. It is particularly useful when dealing with large and complex access 
control needs. 
From the AVEVA Administration, click Access Control Assistant on the Home tab. 
Important: 
The ACA can be used in Global projects. There are however some limitations on the scope of operations that can 
be done. When administering a satellite, it is not possible to affect Roles or Teams, so Role, Teams and Perops 
cannot be created, deleted or modified. These operations can only be done at the hub. Moreover, when the 
system database at a specific location is not primary, all other operations are disabled too. In this instance, ACA 
acts only as a viewer for access control information. In all cases, ACA doesn't allow any modifications to data 
structures that are read-only at a location in a global project. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1289
ACA Interface 
The Access Control Assistant (ACA) interface is split into a top and bottom panel and all data is clearly arranged 
under four distinct tabs inside each panel. For more on working with panels, please refer to Copy, Move and 
Assign Elements. 
To work on data inside the top or bottom panel, click anywhere inside the panel. Click on any one of the four 
tabs to make your selection: 
• Users - shows Users along with Access Control Rights (ACRs) and ACR groups, and Teams together with Users 
grouped under these Teams, 
• ACRs - shows all ACRs defined in the database along with assigned Scopes and Roles, and ACR Groups, 
• Scopes - shows all defined Scopes, 
• Roles - shows all defined Roles with Permissible Operations (Perops) belonging to those Roles. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1290
For more information on the above terminology, please refer to Data Access Control (DAC). 
The tabs are particularly beneficial as database information can be extensive and viewing everything inside a 
single window can be unwieldy. With the help of tabs, it is possible to view elements of one particular ACR or 
Scope inside its own dedicated tab; it is also convenient when making assignments between each element. 
Each tab contains a tree structure consisting of nodes, where each node represents a database element. Each 
node in turn displays children representing elements related to their parent. 
The following types of database nodes found in the ACA: 
• User - contains children of type ACR or ACR Group representing ACR elements assigned to the User, 
• Team - contains children of type User representing Users involved in the Team, 
• ACR - contains children of types Scope and Role representing assigned access control right elements, 
• ACR Group - contains ACR nodes representing ACRs involved in the group, 
• Scope - nodes of this type do not have any child nodes, 
• Role - contains Perop nodes that the Role consists of, 
• Perop - nodes of this type do not have any child nodes. 
The above relationships between nodes are recursive. This means that when a Team node contains User nodes, 
those nodes in turn, have will have ACR nodes, which again contain Scopes and Roles etc. 
The advantage of this approach is that the user can see all access control elements connected with a specific 
database element in one single view. 
For example, it is possible to view complete information about access rights for a single User including all 
assigned ACRs and ACR Groups with Scope and Role information along with Perop definitions, all in one place. 
Moreover, it is possible to edit this information, singly or collectively. 
Viewing Data 
Click on the arrow head to expand a node and view the cells holding attribute values. Many consecutive nodes of 
the same type form a grid or table as shown in below. 
To sort the data by a column of attributes, click on the column header. It is also possible to rearrange the 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1291
columns for easier viewing, by dragging to new positions. 
Editing Data 
To edit an attribute, double-click on the cell containing the attribute. 
The user can also press the <Enter> key to highlight and move between selections. To edit a selected cell, press 
Enter again. Press the <Esc> key to move the selection to a higher level, for example, from a cell to a parent 
node. 
When the user edits data in a cell, the corresponding attribute in the selected database element is also modified. 
Most operations can be performed with a context menu. To open the context menu, select one or more nodes 
and click the right mouse button. The user can also perform operations on a group of nodes by selecting multiple 
nodes in the same grid. 
Primary Nodes 
A single database element can occur in multiple trees in different tabs. For example, a node representing a 
particular Access Control Right (ACR) can occur in the list of all ACRs as an ACR assigned to a User or inside an 
ACR Group. The original created element is designated as the Primary Node. 
To show the Primary Node, right click on any node to display the context menu. Select Go to primary node to 
highlight the particular Primary Node. This will display the parent tab and tree in the active panel. 
Note: 
All Permissible Operation (Perop) nodes are Primary as they are always displayed in the context of their parent 
Role. 
Create and Delete Elements 
The easiest way to create a new access control element is to select a primary node for an existing element and 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1292
choose Duplicate from the context menu. The node is copied with all its attributes and child nodes. 
To create a blank (empty) element, select one of the root nodes for Users, Access Control Right (ACRs), ACR 
Groups, Scopes or Roles, and select an item from the context menu, for example, "New user". 
Note: 
New user creates a project user; for this user to be able to log in to the project a password must be set. Refer to 
Modify Attributes of Users and Teams. 
To remove any element including its hierarchy of descendants, simply select a primary node representing this 
element and choose the Delete option from the context menu. The user can also use the <DEL> key on the 
keyboard. 
Deleting an element also deletes it from any other nodes in which it occurs. To retain the original element, but 
remove separate instances, use the Unassign option. For more on unassigning elements, refer to Unassigning. 
Copy, Move and Assign Elements 
One of the most important operations in the ACA is assignment. The user can assign: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1293
• Users to Teams, 
• Access Control Rights (ACRs) or ACR Groups to Users, 
• ACRs to ACR Groups, 
• Roles or Scopes to ACRs. 
The easiest way to make an assignment is to click the source node inside a panel, drag it with the mouse and 
drop it onto the destination node in the other panel. This is where the concept of two identical panels is very 
helpful. 
When the user drags-and-drops a Permissible Operations (Perop) onto another Role, the Perop is moved to the 
destination. To make a copy of the Perop, hold down the <CTRL> key while dragging and dropping. 
When dragging a primary node to a destination node, (for instance, a Scope onto an ACR) the user makes an 
assignment. However, when the user drags a secondary node to a destination node, they are moving an already 
established assignment from one node to another. 
Clipboard Operations 
All operations that can be done with the mouse using a drag-and-drop technique can also be done on the 
keyboard using the windows clipboard. Select a set of nodes and choose Copy or Cut from the context menu (or 
press <Ctrl+C> or <Ctrl+X> combinations respectively). This copies the nodes to the clipboard. Select Paste on 
the destination node (or press <Ctrl+V>) to copy the nodes into the destination. 
Note: 
By selecting Copy the user performs an assignment or move operation, depending on the context. By selecting 
Cut the user performs a move operation. 
Unassigning 
The user can remove an established assignment from a non-primary node by selecting the Unassign option from 
the context menu. Using this option the user can: 
• Remove a User from a Team. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1294
• Unassign an Access Control Right (ACR) or ACR Group from a User. 
• Remove an ACR from an ACR Group. 
• unassign a Role or Scope from an ACR. 
The user can also use the <DEL> key on the keyboard to unassign elements. 
Reverse Assignments 
As the name suggests, some assignments can be done the other way round. For example, a single Scope is 
usually assigned to a single Access Control Right (ACR). If however, the user wants to assign a number of ACRs to 
a single Scope, they can use the concept of reverse assignments. 
To do this, select the multiple ACRs and drag-and-drop them onto the Scope. The Scope will be now assigned to 
all the selected ACRs. 
With the reverse assignment option, it is possible to assign: 
• Teams to Users (e.g. to add a User to a number of Teams in single operation). 
• Users to ACRs or ACR Groups. 
• ACR Groups to ACR (e.g. to add an ACR to multiple groups at once) 
• ACRs to Roles or Scopes. 
Queries 
The Access Control Assistant (ACA) provides additional powerful capabilities through the mechanism of queries. 
Using queries, the user can examine specific sets of elements. 
To make a query, select a node, right click on the node to open the context menu and select Show [type of 
query]. For example, Show users assigned to this ACR group. 
The following types of queries are available: 
• For User nodes - query Teams containing the selected User. 
• For Access Control Right (ACR) nodes - query Users having the selected ACR assigned, query ACR Groups 
containing the selected ACR. 
• For Scope nodes - query ACRs having the selected Scope assigned. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1295
• For Role nodes - query ACRs having the selected Role assigned. 
• Query Roles that control access to specific element types. This query is available at the root Roles node and 
it lets the user specify a list of types for which the query is being made. 
User Interface for Queries 
When you initiate a query inside one panel, a new tab opens in the other panel. 
Query tabs are placed in a separate group on the tab list. These tabs can be moved inside and across panels by 
dragging the tab headers. To close the Query tabs, click on the cross or use the <Ctrl+W> key combination in the 
tab. 
To view all available tabs, use the drop-down menu on right-hand side of the panel header. 
Query Changes 
Each Query tab contains results of the performed query. These results are dynamic, meaning the query 
information automatically changes to reflect any change in the database. 
The most powerful aspect of queries in Access Control Assistant (ACA) is that they are active queries. In addition 
to viewing the results of your query, the user can also modify the information shown. Any such modification 
done to the query results will immediately be updated in the database. 
Note: 
Changes to queries for Roles are not allowed, as results of this query are determined in a one-way operation. 
Multi-Disciplinary Project Data 
Multi-Disciplinary Project Data provides the administrator with Controlled Object Revisioning (COR) functionality. 
COR is used to control when and if users are alerted to changes to objects made by disciplines other than their 
own. When such changes are made, and the objects are released via status control, a notification is sent to any 
users that have subscribed to that object. Users may then decide if and when to adopt the change for their own 
discipline. 
For example, in a typical Datasheet a number of attributes from different disciplines provide the user with an 
overview of an item. The user will need to modify attributes within their discipline, and also want to see 
attributes from other disciplines. When attributes from the other disciplines change, the user will want to control 
when they see those changes. COR enables this to be implemented by deploying an extract hierarchy that allows 
users to choose when to pick up status changes from other disciplines. 
An extract hierarchy is used to support an object lifecycle (for example, Issued > Approval > Working) and release 
mechanism, and in addition provide a number of consumer extracts. These consumer extracts enable users from 
other disciplines to pull changes into their own extract hierarchy and view. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1296
Within a discipline, items are promoted through the status of the define lifecycle, e.g. typically from Working to 
Approval and finally to Issued. Upon reaching the Issued status, the system sends a notification to users that 
have subscribed to the object inform them that changes have been and are available to them. The users can 
optionally choose whether or not to adopt the change. 
Inter-Discipline Project Setup 
It is the responsibility of Project Administrators to set up COR for projects, ensuring that the required extract 
hierarchies, Teams and Multiple Databases (MDBs) are available. An Inter-Discipline Project Setup feature is 
provided to help Administrators do this. The feature partially automates the setting up of the required teams, 
databases, MDBs etc in accordance with user-defined parameters. 
Other objects that are required to be setup for COR are Disciplines and Maturity levels. 
Disciplines are the engineering disciplines (Piping, Electrical, etc) required for Inter-Discipline Project set-up. 
Maturity levels determine the status approval workflow, or items, e.g. Issued > Approval > Working. When the 
automation process is run an extract hierarchy will be created for each discipline, with consumer extracts also 
generated for each discipline extract hierarchy other than itself. 
Global Inter-Discipline Project Setup 
Global Interdisciplinary Project Setup is supported: setup can be configured for a single location, either the Hub 
or a Satellite. 
For the Hub, project automation can be undertaken as a single process; however for Satellites the process needs 
to be divided into Hub operations and Satellite operations. 
Teams, databases and extract hierarchies must be created at the Hub. These databases and extracts are then 
allocated to the appropriate Satellite. The Satellite automation process generates MDBs to be used at that 
location. 
Interdisciplinary Project Setup can be run without databases being allocated to the Satellite, but the global DB 
must be propagated before the process can generate its own MDBs. 
The workflow for Interdisciplinary Project Setup for a Satellite is: 
At the Hub: 
• Create Disciplines. 
• Create Maturities. 
• Create Locations. 
• Run Interdisciplinary Project Setup at the Hub. 
• The system creates Teams, Databases and Extracts. 
• If the Satellite is initialized, allocate Databases (DBs) to it. 
At the Satellite: 
• Define the DB Common Set. 
• Run the Interdisciplinary Project Setup function to create the MDBs 
Common Databases 
Databases that are not created as part of the Interdisciplinary Project Setup need to be included in MDBs in 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1297
order to provide access Tags data. The relevant Dictionary databases must be included in MDBs with the Tags 
data. Design and other databases may also be required. 
It is possible to create a DB Set to hold these Databases which will be referenced at Project Setup time. The DB 
Set is created using existing Administration DB Set functions, and should contain only those Databases that are to 
be included in the MDBs. The order of the databases in the DB Set is reflected in the order of databases in each 
MDB. 
Baselines 
Baselines are views of a project consisting of all data from all disciplines saved at a particular state. Disciplines 
can continue to change data and create new revisions of data without changing the project views saved to 
baselines. Baselines may be created at any time after the initial project configuration has been defined. In 
practice they are likely to be created at key project milestones. 
Create Disciplines 
Discipline objects represent engineering disciplines. The attributes of tagged items will be divided between 
multiple disciplines, each group of discipline attributes to be editable by users within the discipline in question. 
Note: 
New disciplines can be added to a set of levels after interdisciplinary project creation. Refer to Update or Delete 
Project Data for details. 
To create a discipline, click Disciplines (ENGI) on the Manage tab and click Create. The Create Discipline window 
is displayed: 
Enter the unique identifier of the new Discipline in the Name field. The field does not support spaces. They will 
be automatically removed if entered. 
Enter a "user-friendly" descriptive name for the Discipline in the Display Name field. 
In the Code (Required) field enter a unique code for the Discipline. This will be used in automatically generated 
team, database and Multiple Database (MDB) names. Cannot contain symbols or spaces. If a Discipline Code is 
used in team names, it must consist of alphabetic characters only. 
Enter a description in the Description field. 
The entry in the DB Range Start field is to determine the start point for databases numbers required for 
Discipline. When the Inter-Discipline Project Setup is run it will use the first number higher than the entered start 
range as the Database (DB) Number for the extract hierarchy. 
In the DB Range Start field enter the lowest number that will be applied to databases created for this discipline. 
The system will use the first unused DB Number greater than or equal to the given number for databases created 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1298
for this discipline. The number can be set to 0, in which case and the system will find the lowest available valid 
DB Number. It can be set to any valid DB number even if it is already in use or is shared by other disciplines. 
A warning is issued if the user selects a database number in the range already claimed for databases in AVEVA 
standard projects. (7000 to 8000 and 250000 to 255000). This does not prevent users from using a number in this 
range however. 
Click System to complete the DB Range Start field with a system selected DB Number. 
Click Project to select the next available DB Number in the project-specific DB Range. 
Note: 
If Project functionality is unavailable for selection this means that the project does not contain a project-specific 
DB Range. 
Click Apply. The discipline will be created and added to the list of disciplines . 
Repeat the above procedure until all required disciplines have been created. For example: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1299
Modify Disciplines 
To modify a discipline, click Disciplines (ENGI) on the Manage tab and click Modify. The Modify Discipline 
window is displayed: 
Amend the properties of the Discipline as required, then click OK or Apply. 
Once an interdisciplinary project including the Discipline has been created, the Discipline Code cannot be 
modified. The other attributes can be changed at any time. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1300
Delete Disciplines 
Note: 
Disciplines can only be deleted if they have not been used when creating a project. 
To delete a discipline, click Disciplines (ENGI) on the Manage tab. Select the discipline to delete and click Delete. 
Confirm that you want to delete the discipline. 
Create Maturity Levels 
Maturity levels define the lifecycle and approval process that items will go through. 
A standard three level approval mechanism of Issued > Approved > Working requires three levels of extracts 
Issued = Master -> Approved = Level 1 Extract -> Working = Level 2 Extract. Items are created and modified in the 
Working second level extract. Once they require Approval, they are FLUSHED into the Approved first level 
extract. Then finally, when they are APPROVED, they are FLUSHED into the Issued Master. 
Note: 
New Maturity levels cannot be added to a set of levels after interdisciplinary project creation. 
To create a set of maturity levels, click Maturity (ENGI) on the Manage tab and click Create. The Maturity
window is displayed: 
Click New. A row of fields is then added to the window in which default values for the new level are displayed. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1301
Modify the default values as required. 
Enter the unique identifier of the new Maturity level in the Name field. The field does not support spaces. They 
will be automatically removed if entered. 
Enter a "user-friendly" descriptive name for the Maturity level in the Display Name field. 
In the Code (Required) field enter a unique code for the Maturity level. This will be used in automatically 
generated team, database and Multiple Database (MDB) names. Cannot contain symbols or spaces. If a Maturity 
level code is used in team names, it must consist of alphabetic characters only. 
Enter a description in the Description field. 
Repeat the above procedure until all required maturity levels have been created. For example: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1302
The order of the maturity levels as listed in the window defines the extract hierarchy, with the top level being the 
Primary (Master) database level. It is possible to create the maturity levels in any order, and then rearrange the 
levels to the correct order by using the up and down arrows on the right-hand side of the window. 
To move a level up and down, select its row and click either the up or down arrow button as required to move it 
up or down one row. 
Note: 
The buttons are disabled while a row is being edited. 
Once the required maturity levels have been entered, exit the window using the X button. 
The Maturity levels are listed in the Maturity window. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1303
Modify Maturity Levels 
To modify maturity levels, click Maturity (ENGI) on the Manage tab and click either Modify or Create. The 
Maturity window displays the existing maturity levels. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1304
Modify the properties of the maturity levels as required. 
Once an interdisciplinary project including the maturity level has been created, the Discipline Code cannot be 
modified. The other attributes can be changed at any time. 
Delete Maturity Levels 
Note: 
Maturity levels can only be deleted if they have not been used in a when creating a project. 
To delete a Maturity level, select it in the Maturity window and click Delete. A message will then be displayed, 
requesting that the deletion of the Maturity level, be confirmed. For example: 
Click Yes to complete the deletion. 
Inter-Discipline Project Creation 
To run the inter-disciplinary project facility, select Inter Disciplinary Project Data > Manage on the Tools tab. 
The Interdisciplinary Project Data window is displayed: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1305
Note: 
For project creation procedure required at a Global Satellite refer to Inter-Discipline Project Creation at a Global 
Satellite. For non-Global projects and for Global Hubs, follow the procedure described below. 
1. In the Base Name field, enter a code to identify the project definition. It will be used in naming conventions 
for the databases and Multiple Databases (MDBs) that are to be linked to this project definition. 
2. The Disciplines section lists all the Disciplines (created as described in Create Disciplines). 
3. The Maturity section lists all the Maturity levels (created as described in Create Maturity Levels). 
4. The Global section of the window is only displayed when running Administration at a Global Hub. From the 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1306
Location list, select the target location for project creation. The MDB will be created at the selected location, 
and all databases created at the location will be "primary". 
If a Satellite is selected then the Allocate Databases check box will be enabled. If the check box is checked 
then the databases will be allocated to the target location immediately after they have been created at the 
Hub. Otherwise any database allocation will have to be performed by the System Administrator using the 
standard database distribution and allocation facilities. 
It is not possible to change the location after the databases have been created. 
5. To add a common Database Set to the project definition, click Select in the Common Databases section. The 
Select Common DB Set window is then displayed: 
The window lists all common Database Sets (created as described in Database Sets). Select the required set 
and click OK. The selected set is then displayed in the Common Databases section. 
Only one set can be selected at a time. If a Database Set was previously selected, it will be replaced. 
6. To view details of the selected Database Set, click Show Detail in the Common Databases section. The 
Database List for Common Database Set window is then displayed: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1307
The window displays details of the databases in the set. 
7. To remove the selected Database Set from the project definition, click Clear in the Common Databases 
section. 
8. The Naming Convention section displays the naming conventions for team, database and Multiple Database 
(MDB) names, for example, the elements that they are made up from. This is displayed for information only 
and cannot be amended. 
9. Click Create Project Data to create the project, teams, databases etc. Project definition MDBs are also 
created in a non-Global project or at the Hub for Global projects (for Satellites, they must be created 
separately at the Satellite location, as described in Inter-Discipline Project Creation at a Global Satellite). 
During project creation, any information regarding the progress of the procedure and any error messages are 
displayed in the Summary section of the window. A Details link will provide access to a text log file of all the 
messages. 
A team is created for each combination of Discipline and Maturity. Teams are created for consumer extracts. 
A primary Database is created for each Discipline. Database extracts are then created for the main Maturity 
workflow and for the consumer extracts. 
If MDBs are being generated, they are created for each combination of Discipline and Maturity. 
Inter-Discipline Project Creation at a Global Satellite 
For global projects, as in explain in Global Inter-Discipline Project Setup, inter-discipline project creation is 
divided into processes that must be undertaken at the Hub, and those that must be undertaken at Satellites. 
Teams, databases and extract hierarchies must be created at the Hub (Inter-Discipline Project Creation). These 
databases and extracts are then allocated to the appropriate Satellite. Multiple Database (MDBs) must then be 
generated at the Satellite to be used at that location. 
Note: 
It is possible to run Interdisciplinary Project Setup at a Satellite before the databases and Extracts have been 
allocated. As long as the Satellite has the latest version of the Global Database (DB), the MDBs can be predefined 
prior to allocation. However, no user will be able to access the MDBs until all Databases and Extracts have been 
allocated. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1308
Select Utilities > Interdisciplinary Project Data > Manage from the main menu. The Interdisciplinary Project 
Data window is then displayed. When accessed from a Satellite, it appears as follows: 
Except as described below, all information displayed on the window is for information only and cannot be 
changed at a Satellite. 
Common Database Sets can be added to the project definition of a Satellite until the MDBs have been created, 
using the facilities of the COMMON DATABASES section. Refer to Inter-Discipline Project Creation for details. 
To create the MDBs for the Satellite, click Create Satellite MDBs. 
Any information regarding the progress of the procedure and any error messages are displayed in the Summary
section of the window. A Details link will provide access to a text log file of all the messages. 
Update or Delete Project Data 
To update or delete project data, access the Interdisciplinary Project Data window by selecting Utilities > 
Interdisciplinary Project Data > Manage from the main menu. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1309
After project creation, the base name, location and common databases cannot be changed. The date and time of 
project creation is displayed at the top of the window, next to the Base Name field. 
The window displays lists of Disciplines and Maturity levels for review purposes, as before project creation. In 
the case of Discipline, a green tick is displayed next to those disciplines for which has been defined for the 
project. 
Disciplines created after project creation (Create Disciplines) will therefore be displayed without a green tick. To 
define project data for these Disciplines, click Update Project Data. 
If there are no new Disciplines, a warning is displayed to inform the user that all project data has already been 
created. Otherwise, the required project data (Teams etc.) is then created. 
Delete Project Data 
Click Delete Project Data. 
A project definition can be deleted if no data has been stored in any of the its databases. In a Global project, 
databases can only be deleted at the Hub, and only if these databases have not allocated to a Satellite location. 
Baselines 
Baselines are views of a project consisting of all data from all disciplines saved at a particular state. 
New Baselines can be added at any time, but extract databases linked to a Baseline can only be created after 
Interdisciplinary Project Databases have been created. 
To configure Baselines, select Utilities > Inter Disciplinary Project Data > Baseline from on the Tools tab. The 
Baseline window is displayed: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1310
The window lists any Baselines that have already been set up. 
The Baseline Databases section initially base name that will be used to generate the names of Baseline 
databases and Multiple Databases (MDBs), and the Baseline Team, as described below. The date and time that 
the Interdisciplinary Project data was created is also displayed. 
To create a baseline, click New. The new baseline is then created and listed in the window. For example: 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1311
A row of fields is added to the window in which default values for the new Baseline are displayed. Modify the 
default values as required. 
For a Global project, Baselines are created at the target location as specified during the interdisciplinary project 
creation process (Inter-Discipline Project Creation). 
Enter the unique identifier of the new Baseline in the Name field. The field does not support spaces. They will be 
automatically removed if entered. 
In the ID (Required) field enter a unique code for the Baseline. This will be used in automatically generated 
database and MDB names. It must be unique and must not contain symbols or spaces. 
Enter a description in the Description field. 
The Created field displays the date and time that the session in which the Baseline was created was saved to the 
database (not the date and time that the New was clicked to create the Baseline). This cannot be modified by the 
user. 
To create a Team (if Baseline Databases have not been created before), Databases and MDBs for Baselines, select 
it and click Create Databases. 
During database creation, any information regarding the progress of the procedure and any error messages are 
displayed in the Baseline Databases section of the window. A Details link will provide access to a text log file of 
all the messages. 
Once databases etc. have been created for a Baseline, a green tick is displayed next to its row in the window. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1312
The first time that Baseline databases are created, a Team is created with a name of <base name>BL. This Team 
is used by all baselines, so it is only created when the first Baseline databases are created. 
Extract databases for each Discipline that will be included in the MDB for that Baseline are created (there are no 
Primary DB or maturity level extracts for a Baseline). Database names are formatted as follows: <Base 
name>_<Discipline code>_<Baseline ID>. 
An MDB for the Baseline is also created with a name of <Base name>_<Baseline ID>_BL. The MDB contains the 
Baseline Extracts and the databases from the common database set used in the Discipline MDBs. 
For a Global project, databases are created at the target location as specified during the interdisciplinary project 
creation process. If the target location is the hub, the MDB is created there as well. 
If the target location is a Satellite, the MDB must be created at that location. Access the Baseline window at the 
location. Instead of Create Databases, a Create Databases will be displayed. Click this to create the MDB at the 
Satellite location. 
Modify Baselines 
To modify Baselines, select Inter Disciplinary Project Data > Baseline on the Tools tab. The Baseline displays the 
existing Baselines. 
Amend the properties of the baselines as required. 
Once databases for a Baseline have been created, the ID cannot be modified. The other attributes can be 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1313
changed at any time. 
Delete Baselines 
Baselines can be deleted at any time before databases have been created for them. After databases have been 
created for them, Baselines cannot be deleted if any of these databases have been used (for example, there are 
user created sessions in one or more of them). 
If databases have been created for a Baseline but not used, they will be deleted along with the Baseline. The 
Multiple Database (MDB) will also be deleted, but the Team for Baselines will not be. 
To delete a Baseline, select it in the Baseline window and click Delete. The selected Baseline (and associated 
databases) is then immediately deleted. 
Tag Management System 
To run the tag management system configuration facility, select Utilities > Tag Management System from the 
main menu. The Tag Management System Configuration window is displayed. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1314
The functionality available from the Tag Management System Configuration window is identical to that 
described in Inter-Discipline Project Creation functionality with the exception of the global functionality, refer to 
Inter-Discipline Project Creation for further information. 
TMS on Global 
TMS can operate in an AVEVA Global environment. It is possible to set up a TMS-enabled project as a Global 
project, such that TMS managed tags can be created and updated by a TMS service running at the hub. The new 
and updated tags are sent back immediately to the requesting location. The updates are propagated to other 
locations using scheduled global updates (update events). Refer to Global documentation for help on setting up a 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1315
Global project. 
Once a TMS-enabled project has been made global, the following actions are needed to set up the TMS service 
and databases 
• Install the Engineering Service (TMS) at the hub 
• Install and configure Shared Services at each location 
• Allocate the TMS primary database to all locations. This must remain primary at the hub. 
• Create a separate TMS consumer extract for each location and allocate it as primary at the location. 
Creating TMS Consumer Extracts for Locations 
Create an extract database from the TMS primary database, assigned to the appropriate team (TMS consumer 
extracts typically have their own team, TMSCX). Navigate to the new extract database in the explorer and run the 
command to make the extract a consumer extract: 
> LConsumer true 
Allocate and add the consumer extracts to the required Engineering Multiple Databases (MDBs) at the locations. 
The default TMS consumer extract can be used for clients running at the hub but should be deallocated from any 
other satellites. There should be only one TMS consumer extract at any location. 
Import and Export Utilities 
While setting up a new project the user might want to use teams, users, databases and other resources that 
were created in a previous project. Instead of setting them up manually the AVEVA Administration export utility 
provides an easy way of exporting all the data into a Microsoft Excel spreadsheet where it can be stored (and 
also modified) for future use. The exported document can be easily imported into another project using the 
import utility. 
Options for Export, Import and Rollback are displayed on the Home tab: 
Import Config 
Used for importing project data from a specified 
Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. 
Export Config 
Used for exporting project data into a specified 
Microsoft Excel spreadsheet. 
Rollback Config 
Used to undo all the changes made to a project by the 
last Import operation. The Rollback option is only 
visible if an Import operation has been completed. 
The following table lists all of the elements within a database that are transferred during an export or import 
operation:

Specify a full path and filename for the new Excel file to export to. 
Click OK to begin the export process.

Passwords will not be written to the exported spreadsheet. After importing the Administrator of the new project 
will have to set user passwords before the imported users can log in. 
The Exporting to Excel progress report will be displayed:

Spreadsheet Data Format 
For Spreadsheet Data Format information refer to Related Topics. 
Default Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet 
By default the Export tool creates a Microsoft Excel file containing 12 sheets named as follows: 
Teams 
Users 
Authenticated Users 
Databases 
Foreign Includes 
Extracts 
Working Extracts 
Multiple Databases (MDBs) 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1321
Access Control Rights (ACRs) 
ACR Groups 
Scopes 
Roles and Permissible Operations (Perops) 
Where multiple MDBs exist in the project, there will be an Excel sheet for each MDB. 
The first row in every sheet is a comment line containing column descriptions. A comment line starts with a hash 
character (#) and is ignored by the import tool. 
Although this is the default layout, it is possible to have any number of the sheets deleted or renamed (for 
example all the data could be held by only one sheet). 
Spreadsheet Structure 
Although the import utility is flexible and allows customisation of the spreadsheet, there is a convention that 
needs to be followed for the data to be valid. 
The first column (column A in a sheet) of every row that contains data (except for comment lines) must contain a 
keyword. All keywords are case sensitive. 
Keyword 
Data Type 
TEAM 
Team 
USER 
User 
AUTHUSER 
Authenticated User 
DATABASE 
Database 
COPYFOREIGN 
Foreign Copy 
FOREIGN 
Foreign Include 
EXTRACT 
Extract 
WORKEXTRACT 
Working Extract 
MDB 
Multiple Database (MDB) 
ACR 
Access Control Right (ACR) 
ACRGROUP 
ACR Group 
SCOPE 
Scope 
ROLE 
Role 
PEROP 
Permissible Operatio (Perop) 
A row can also be used as a comment row. If that is the case then first populated cell in the row needs to begin 
with hash character (#), it does not need to be a cell in column A in the Excel sheet. 
If a row does not begin with a keyword and is not a comment line then it is invalid and the Import operation will 
not be successful.

Global Projects 
In the case of a global project all the data from the system and global databases is output during export even if it 
is read-only. Only Foreign Copy rows cannot be produced during export - they cannot be exported in any type of 
projects. 
There are, however, limitations on what kind of data is possible to import depending on current configuration. 
AVEVA Administration export and import will ignore the types that cannot be imported and the user will be 
warned. Teams, Databases, Extracts, Roles and Permissible Operations (Perops) can be imported only at the hub 
and will be ignored at satellites. All the other data types can be imported only if the system database is primary 
at currently administered location. 
Modules 
The modules are set up in the supplied product, and the user will not normally need to create or edit module 
definitions in order to run the AVEVA base product. The only part of the definition that the user may want to 
change is the initialization macro (imacro ), which is run on entry to the module. The initialization macro 
typically contains module-specific commands to set up the screen display, including loading application macros. 
How Modules are Defined 
Each module has an entry in the System database, which consists of the MODULE command followed by 
commands to specify: 
• The security for the module: 
General modules can be accessed by General users; 
Restricted modules can only be accessed by Free users; 
Free modules can be accessed by any user. 
• The runfile. 
• The initialisation macro (imacro), which is run on entry to the module. 
• The Read/Write access to database types. For example, MODEL needs Write access to MODEL database, but 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1329
Read access to Catalogue databases. 
• The buffer size . 
Module Definition 
The Module Definition window is displayed when the user selects Module Definitions on the Home tab. This 
window allows the user to create and edit modules definitions for the current project. 
The Modules pane lists the AVEVA base product modules. 
The Sort by gadget controls the order in which modules are displayed in the list. 
The Settings pane displays the settings for the module selected in the Modules list. You can change all of these 
except the Number and Name. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1330
Security can be set to Restricted, General or Free. 
Runfile sets the file used to start up the module. The defaults are supplied in the %AVEVA_DESIGN_EXE% 
directory. 
Imacro sets the file used to start up the GUI for the module. The default is given by the %ADMSTART% variable. 
Advanced Settings displays the Advanced Module Settings window. This window allows the user to set the buffer 
size (Setting the DABACON Buffer Size), the default database type and access for the module. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1331
Note: 
Changing the options on this window may cause serious problems when using the AVEVA base product. The 
window is for expert use only. 
Set the DABACON Buffer Size 
Each module definition in the System DB may include a BUFFER command. This command specifies how much 
space is to be reserved for the Database Constructor (DABACON) buffer in each module, and allows the database 
constructor to trade off CPU usage against disk I/O to some extent and thereby optimize computer efficiency in 
some of the more heavily used modules. 
If the module definition does not include a buffer size, the default buffer size of 5,120,000 words (20MB) is used. 
The maximum is size is 51,200,000 words (200MB). The modmac macro sets the buffer size for all modules 
(except the Global daemon) to 12,800,000 words (50MB); the user is advised to leave it at this. 
If the environment variable PDMSBUF is defined by a user, this overrides the value from the BUFFER command ‑ 
this can be used to change the amount of memory used on machines with particularly large or small amounts of 
free memory. 
Users are advised to contact AVEVA Solutions Limited if they have any doubts about the setting of their buffer 
sizes. 
The syntax used to set up the size of a buffer in AVEVA Administration is either: 
BUFFER integer(where integer is the required buffer size in words) 
when the module definition is being created (for example, in the project module definition macro) or: 
EDIT MODULE moduleid BUFFER integer 
where an existing module definition is being edited. integer is the number of words to be reserved for the buffer. 
The buffer can be reset to its default value at any time. For example: 
ED MOD ISODRAFT BUF DEF 
would reset the Isodraft buffer size to its default value. 
Database Upgrade 
Managing projects with 2 versions of software requires some thought and consideration depending on the 
product being used. Please check the release documents for each product for any special conditions. The 
DBUPGRADE process does not transform database content. It sets a flag on the database that stops earlier 
software versions being used. 
When the DBUPGRADE command is issued on a database or whole project, it is not possible to Undo that 
transaction, so it is strongly recommended a full back-up is in place should customers need to return any 
database(s) to the previous software version. 
Part Upgrades 
Each individual change of the data model between two different software releases is referred to here as a Part 
Upgrade. In general these Part Upgrades have been designed so that new software releases can work with a 
database that has not been upgraded and the software will degrade gracefully - that is, the software will 
continue to work, although new functionality may not be fully present nor will it be possible to write data into 
these databases (unless the Part Upgrade is optional, The Upgrade Process). This means that it is possible for 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1332
users to continue to work with Foreign DBs, which may be shared with earlier projects and which have not been 
upgraded, included in their projects. An example would be a Corporate Catalogue Database used for earlier and 
multiple projects. 
A framework is provided to run all the part upgrades. The user is provided with a single upgrade command, an all 
or nothing approach, which they can execute. As a consequence of the 'all or nothing' approach, the project 
must be returned to its original state if any part of the upgrade fails. 
Upgrade Framework 
The upgrade process will put an upgrade number in databases, indicating the level to which they have been 
upgraded. This will make it easy to detect on opening whether a database has or has not been upgraded. This 
upgrade number will also be used by the Reconfigure. 
Part upgrades outside the Framework: 
• Are independent of all other non-framework upgrades (that means, non-framework upgrades can be applied 
in any order 
• Have a method of determining whether or not they have been applied, not relying on the upgrade number 
• This to be available to the user 
It will not be possible to backtrack to pre-upgrade sessions. 
Mandatory/Optional Upgrade 
The upgrade process enters an upgrade number in databases, indicating the level to which they have been 
upgraded. This will make it easy to detect, on opening, whether a database has or has not been upgraded. There 
are two variants of upgrades, mandatory or optional. An upgrade number represents either a mandatory 
upgrade or an optional upgrade. 
Mandatory upgrade means that the database requires an upgrade for software being able to write to the 
database. 
Optional upgrade means that the software manages to write to the database even though not upgraded. This 
means that new software versions provided with an optional upgrade facilitates a graceful deployment and 
installation as such a version very well can operate in a mixed environment with previous software versions. 
However, new software does not share the benefits the Parts Upgrade provides until the optional upgrade is 
applied. A soon as applied, the database is no longer accessible from previous software versions. 
Note: 
A gradual upgrade, database per database also enables for a graceful upgrade process, not all databases in a 
project need to be upgraded at once. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1333
Global 
Each Database (DB) must be entirely in either an upgraded or non-upgraded state for the software to operate. 
Therefore it is necessary that all extracts of a DB are processed during an upgrade. 
The granularity of an upgrade will be a Project, excluding Foreign DBs. 
Upgrade Commands 
There is a single upgrade command which will work on a Database (DB) or the whole project. If successful, the 
upgrade number for the DB will be updated. 
The suggested syntax is: 
DBUP PROJECT TO LATEST 
DBUP SYSTEM TO LATEST 
DBUP GLOBAL TO LATEST 
DBUP DB team/dbname TO LATEST 
DBUP DB team/dbname TO LATEST OPTIONAL 
The user can replace LATEST with a known upgrade number which can be found using the Q UPGRADE LIST 
command. 
DBUP PROJECT TO 12010101 
Internally the code will invoke all upgrades to get to the required upgrade number. If the upgrade number is 
omitted, then it will be upgraded to the latest. 
Any extracts will be refreshed as part of an upgrade when their primary database is upgraded. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1334
Q UPGRADE STATUS 
This command lists the current upgrade version of all databases in the project and the upgrade version that the 
software works on. If databases are on a lower upgrade version than the software, then the "upgrade required" 
text accompanies the database. 
Q UPGRADE LIST 
This command lists all the part upgrades ("item:" in the response) organized per upgrade version. I.e. a part 
upgrade belongs to a particular upgrade version. An upgrade version is the increment we do database upgrades 
in. The upgrade version is a 8-digit number. So to upgrade a database to a specific upgrade version, the user can 
give the command 
DBUP DB MYTEAM/MYDB TO 12010103 
This command will upgrade the database MYTEAM/MYDB to upgrade version 12010103 including the versions 
12010100, 12010101, 12010102. I.e. upgrades versions are applied sequentially, and it is not possible to skip any 
intermediate versions. 
If any of the upgrade versions listed, are of the type optional, that is noted by a short remark in the list. 
Global Projects 
In Global projects, databases must be upgraded at their primary location. The upgrade must be run separately at 
each project location, since any secondary databases will be ignored. 
All descendant extracts must be primary at the same location as their primary database, otherwise the database 
hierarchy will not be upgraded. Such databases can be identified using the ISEXCP attribute. If a database is 
primary (ISPRIM TRUE), but not all its extracts are primary (ISEXCP FALSE), then it will be omitted from a 
project upgrade. 
Additional syntax is available in Global projects to allow for centrally administered system databases. These 
cannot be upgraded at the administered location, but must be upgraded at their primary location: 
DBUP SYSTEM FOR locnam TO LATEST 
DBUP ALLSYSTEM TO LATEST 
Where locnam defines the LOCID, name or reference (gid) of a Location element in a Global project. This syntax 
will be available in AVEVA Administration. 
The ALLSYSTEM option in a Global project allows all primary system databases to be upgraded. 
Individual satellite system databases may be upgraded using the 'SYSTEM FOR locnam' syntax provided they are 
primary. If the Global daemon is running, the upgrade will issue Global commands to send such administered 
system databases back to the administered locations. 
It is the responsibility of the System administrator to make sure that updates are run to send all modified 
databases to satellites; and to relocate extract databases as required back to their original primary locations. 
In a Global project, the UPGRADE STATUS query will also show the status of secondary databases and extract 
hierarchies. This will help administrators to identify which extracts will need relocating. 
Note: 
Extract hierarchies which contain secondary extracts cannot be upgraded. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1335
The Upgrade Process 
The upgrade process will be undertaken by System Administrators responsible for the project at all locations. It is 
feasible that system administration may be taken at a remote location for some locations. When upgrading 
multiple projects then many System Administrators will need to co-ordinate. The upgrade process may become 
complicated if running through different time zones. The upgrade process will upgrade one project at a time. 
Consideration to the order of projects to be upgraded will need to be undertaken by the user. 
The projects will need to be locked for the duration of the upgrade, with all Users out of the system. 
The following upgrade steps must be performed by an administrator: 
1. Make sure all users have exited from project. 
2. Lock project at all locations (upgrade will check for this). 
3. Disable Automatic update events. 
4. Expunge all users in the system at the local location. 
5. Flush data from Working extracts - these will not be considered. 
6. Check for No Transient Databases. 
7. Data Integrity Check (DICE) project. 
8. If DICE reveals issues, address them, then re-run DICE. 
Administrator may want to unlock project while DICE issues are being addressed, but will need to exclude all 
users and Lock project again before final DICE.] 
9. [After clean DICE]. 
10. Back-up project at all locations 
The following upgrade steps will performed by the upgrade: 
1. At each location, run Update 
2. Deep Refresh with Propagation on all Databases (DBs) 
3. Temporarily relocate all non-Foreign DBs, to make appear Primary at Hub. 
4. Loop over all non-Foreign DBs in project at Hub and Upgrade (i.e. run each framework part-upgrade on that 
DB) 
5. Do not perform SAVEWORK during this process. 
6. Update at all locations. 
7. Refresh. 
8. Post-process at all locations. 
9. Optionally Merge Sessions. 
10. Optionally Reconfigure for Unicode. 
11. Update at all locations. 
12. Relocate DBs back to original locations. 
13. DICE check project. 
14. Perform non-framework upgrades if applicable 
The mechanism by which the Administrator will tell the upgrade whether to Merge Sessions, and whether to 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1336
Reconfigure for Unicode, are design details which will be described in the design documentation. 
Locking the Project 
The project as a whole cannot be locked, only individual locations; however, it is possible to lock all online 
locations from the HUB through Global. To do this run the following command from the HUB: 
LOCK AT <location> 
The HUB can be locked without the need for a daemon command by just typing: 
LOCK 
It is possible to confirm whether locations are locked by evaluating the return result from: 
QUERY LOCK AT <location> 
Extract Hierarchies 
It should not be necessary to change the extract hierarchy, or to consolidate data within extract hierarchies. 
Therefore the System Administrator should not need to FLUSH, ISSUE, DROP data between extracts (working 
extracts are an exception to this). Nor should they need to delete any extract families to leave only Masters. 
However all extracts will need to be relocated to a single location, although this does not need to be the HUB. 
Working Extracts 
The upgrade process will need to make sure that all data is up to date at the HUB where pre-scan data checks 
will need to be made. Working Extracts cannot be propagated as they are specific to a single location. As a result 
all data must be flushed, and claims released from the Working Extract into its parent. This is only true for 
working extracts, all other extracts do not need to be flushed, or have its claims released, as all non working 
extracts will be available at the HUB. 
Offline Locations 
Global supports Offline locations; therefore we cannot assume that the Hub has a Global connection to that 
location. Where as Offline locations do not support distributed Extracts it can support stand-alone extract 
families. 
It will not be possible to co-ordinate the upgrade from another location if Offline locations are used. Offline 
locations are relatively independent, and can be treated as such. 
User Defined Functions 
It is possible to define your own functions in the application 
Copy the file adminuseraddinscallback.pmlfnc from the product installation folder C:\Program Files\AVEVA\
AdministrationX.X.X\PMLLIB\admin\functions into your PMLLIB folder and re-set the PMLLIB environment 
variable. For example, SET PMLLIB=C:\temp\mypmllib;%PMLLIB%. 
Note: 
32 bit: C:\Program Files(x86)\AVEVA\<product name>\mon.exe 
64 bit: C:\Program Files\AVEVA\<product name>\mon.exe 
When AVEVA Administration is launched, a call is made to the new Programmable Macro Language (PML) 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1337
function adminuseraddinscallback.pmlfnc. 
Edit the adminuseraddinscallback.pmlfnc to add your own options. 
Security for Linked Documents 
Some database types allow elements to have links to external documents and websites. 
To control the types of documents and websites the links can have, the project administrator will need to 
construct an allowlist. Refer to Linked Document Allowlist for further information. 
Note: 
If no Allowlist is configured, no document linking is possible. 
Create a RAM Drive 
RAM drives are reserved space in RAM memory that appear as local drives. The performance of a RAM drive is 
faster than accessing hard drives or SDD drives. 
RAM drives can be used by AVEVA software to improve performance of database operations where faster read 
and write access to files is needed. AVEVA have customized the ImDisk Virtual Disk Driver, a third party open-
source software component, and included it as part of the AVEVA Administration product install. 
Customers report poor performance running activities like Database Integrity Checker (DICE) on large projects. 
Configuring and testing the scenario using a RAM drive gave performance improvements by over a factor of two. 
Create a RAM Drive 
Click RamDisk on the Tools tab. The ImDisk Virtual Disk Drive window is displayed. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1338
From the ImDisk Virtual Disk Drive window, select File > Mount new virtual disk or click the Mount New..
button. The Mount new virtual disk window is displayed. 
Note: 
The layout and content of this form comes from the 3rd party. 
Note: 
RAM drives are constrained by the amount of RAM within the machine. AVEVA E3D requires 3GB of RAM to run. 
Consideration of the needs of applications running on the local machine should be made before allocating RAM 
drive memory. 
AVEVA™ Engineering

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.

Enter an image file name in the Image file field, select a suitable drive letter from the Drive letter drop down list 
and set the size of the virtual disk in the Size of virtual disk field and click OK. A message window is displayed, 
click Format disk. 
The Format Local Disk window is displayed.

In the Volume Label field enter a label name and click Start. A Warning message is displayed.

AVEVA™ Engineering

Page 1343
Project Administration 
The Project Administration topic is an overview of the options on the main menu. They are described in the 
order in which they appear on the menu, reading from left to right. Some of the simpler options are described in 
full, and some of them have references to other chapters, where they are described in more detail. 
Lock the Project Database 
The Project database can be locked by clicking Lock on the Home tab. Locking has no effect on users already 
accessing a project, but it does prevent other people from entering the project until the project is unlocked. 
Locking the project may be necessary, for example, to allow a release of newly approved data or to incorporate 
new versions of the AVEVA base product modules. 
Project Options 
These options are accessible from the Project tab: 
Save Work will save all changes to the System database. 
Get Work will refresh your view of the System database with changes made by any other (ADMIN) users. 
Session Comment will display the Session Comment window. The user can enter text to identify the changes 
made in their current session. The user can see details of previous sessions by using the gadgets on the window 
to move through the sessions. 
Modules/MDB displays a submenu from which the user can select the module they want. For modules with 
graphical user interfaces, the user can choose to load from binary files or macro files. The Select MDB option 
enables the user to switch to a different MDB. 
Exit will save changes to the System database and exit from the AVEVA base product. 
Note: 
The user cannot QUIT (that is, exit without saving changes) from AVEVA Administration. This is to make sure that 
there are no inconsistencies between the actual database files and the record of the databases in the Project 
stored in the System database. 
There are also options to access online help for Administration, to contact AVEVA, and to check for updates to 
the software. 
Version and licensing information is displayed. 
Abnormal Exits 
If the user exits abnormally from the application, for example because of a power failure or system crash, and 
the user lists the database files in the operating system, the user may see files with a .deleted extension. This is 
because when the user does any task that involves deleting a database file, the AVEVA base product copies the 
file to be deleted to a new file with the .deleted extension. 
When the user perform a SAVEWORK, or exit normally which automatically does a SAVEWORK, the .deleted files 
are removed. However, if there is a problem which results in database files being deleted, and an exit occurs 
before the System database can be updated with the changes, the AVEVA base product will rename the .deleted 
files and remove the extension, so that the files will still be available. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1344
Upload On-premises Dabacon Project to CONNECT Project 
The Project Upload utility allows the upload of an existing on-premises Dabacon Project to CONNECT cloud-
based storage. After upload, the project can be associated with one or more edge connectors to make it available 
for use with AVEVA E3D Design and AVEVA Administration. 
This is a one-off upload per on-premises Dabacon Project. 
Compatible foreign/reference projects can either be uploaded or the on-premises projects used. The following 
parts of a Dabacon project are uploaded to CONNECT Cloud Storage: 
• Dabacon files. For example, all files in the project folder APS000. 
• Dabacon project files. For example, all files in the folders APSDFLTS, APSDIA, APSDWG, APSGCD, APSINFO, 
APSISO, APSMAC, APSPIC, APSPSI, APSSTE, APSTPL. 
• Dabacon Reference number information which enables reference numbers to be correctly generated when 
Dabacon elements are created. 
The following do not need to be uploaded: 
• Compatible foreign/reference projects. 
• PML files and other customizations which reside outside of the standard project folders. 
Prerequisites 
Before uploading a project, AVEVA recommends the following: 
• For a Global Dabacon project, all data must be migrated to the hub and all satellites shut down. 
• The database must not be an update (non multiwrite). 
• All users must exit the project. 
• A DICE check must be run on the project. 
Depending on company workflows, either first create an empty Dabacon project using the AVEVA Unified 
Engineering - Spectrum Administration dashboard and then upload the project using the Project Upload utility, 
or create the project directly as part of the upload process using the Project Upload utility. 
CONNECT functionality is available to set up users with privileges to create projects, and other users to upload 
into those projects, but not create them. 
The example displays the AVEVA Unified Engineering - Spectrum Administration dashboard and the available 
projects. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1345
The dashboard displays the status of each project: 
• Ready - the project is uploaded and available for use. 
• Deleting - the project is deleted and not available for use. 
• Pending - An empty Dabacon project is created, a project can be uploaded using the Project Upload utility. 
Upload Project 
To upload the current project to CONNECT, in AVEVA Administration, select Project > Upload to AVEVA Connect 
to display the Upload to AVEVA Connect window. 
Note: 
The functionality is not available if the current project is already uploaded to CONNECT. 
Use existing (pending) Project 
Uploads a project to an empty Dabacon project on the AVEVA Unified Engineering - Spectrum Administration 
dashboard. By default, the Projects list is populated with the first (alphabetically) available pending project 
created on the AVEVA Unified Engineering - Spectrum Administration dashboard. All available pending projects, 
with the same project code as the current project, are available for selection. 
The example displays the AVEVA Unified Engineering - Spectrum Administration dashboard with a pending 
project.

Create Project 
To create a project directly on the AVEVA Unified Engineering - Spectrum Administration dashboard, click + on 
the dashboard. 
Note: 
The necessary CONNECT permissions are required to directly create a project. 
Project code 
Displays the code for the current project and cannot be modified. 
Name 
Input a name for the CONNECT project. 
Description 
Input a description for the CONNECT project. 
AVEVA™ Engineering

© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1347
Upload 
Uploads the current project to the cloud. 
During conversion, a progress bar displays upload information. Large projects can take some time to upload. 
A log file is generated and located in the Windows TEMP Path, for example: D:\Views\TMP\AVEVASpectrum- 
Project\UploadLog-UKCAMD5359-20230210-104837.log 
If the upload process is successful, a message displays. 
The uploaded Dabacon project can be associated with an edge connector in the AVEVA Unified Engineering - 
Spectrum Administration dashboard in preparation for use in AVEVA E3D Design and AVEVA Administration. 
If the upload process is not successful, an error message displays. The issues must be resolved before the project 
can be uploaded and subsequently used in AVEVA E3D Design and AVEVA Administration. 
Download CONNECT Project to On- premises Dabacon Project 
The Project Download utility allows the download of an existing CONNECT cloud-based storage project to an on-
premises Dabacon Project. The entire project structure is rebuilt onto a specified disk under the determined 
folder. 
The download processes each of the project folders differently. 
Databases 
Database files in area folders are downloaded assuming that the database is defined in the system DB. Any 
database files that reside in an area that is not directly referenced by the project are ignored. 
DFLTS, INFO, ISO Folders 
All files in all directories of the DFLTS, INFO and ISO folders are downloaded to the target folder. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1348
Inter DB Macros 
Inter DB macros are defined if they are registered in the misc database and are downloaded into the target 
folder assuming that a reference exists. Files that are not referenced are not downloaded. 
PIC, STE, TPL, DIA Folders 
Picture, Stencil, Template and Diagram folders contain files that are directly referenced from their appropriate 
database. All appropriate databases of a type are scanned to determine which files are referenced from the 
project. Only those files that are referenced are downloaded to the target folder, other files are ignored. 
Download Project 
To download the current project to an on-premises Dabacon Project,in AVEVA Administration, select Project > 
Download from AVEVA Connect from the main menu to display the Download Project window. 
Note: 
The functionality is not available if the current project is already an on-premises Dabacon Project. 
Browse 
Specifies the target folder for the project download. All project folders are downloaded into the selected folder. 
OK 
Downloads the current project to an on-premises Dabacon Project. 
Open the Command Window to display details of the conversion progress. Large projects can take some time to 
download. 
When the conversion is complete, a summary displays in the Command Window. 
AVEVA™ Engineering
Administration
© 2015-2025 AVEVA Group Limited and its subsidiaries. All rights reserved.
Page 1349
Command Syntax 
The command line syntax for project download is: 
SPECTR/UM---DOWN/LOAD---directoryname 
There is a current limitation when downloading CONNECT cloud-based storage projects that contain databases in 
areas. An error message displays. 
The error occurs because the system is checking for the existence of the area environment variable. To 
workaround this, an environment variable must be set for each area where a database resides.

Page 1353
If set, these details are displayed each time the project is entered. They can be reset in the application at any 
time. 
Replicate a Project 
The Replicate Project options on the Tools tab enable the user to replicate the whole project, including all the 
data, or just the structure of the project. 
The Project Data option copies the current project to a new project. Before the user uses this option, they must 
create the project directories and set the environment variables for the copied project. Then enter the new 
project code on the Replicate Project window. 
The Project Data to a directory option copies an on-premises Dabacon Project to a target directory on a disk 
located on the local LAN. 
Note: 
A project must not be replicated outside the AVEVA base product by copying the whole of the Project directory 
to another Project directory. This is because information about the project name is stored inside the DBs 
themselves. 
Note: 
Passwords will not be written to REPLICATE output. After running in the generated macro the Administrator of 
the new project will have to set user passwords before the replicated users can log in. 
The Project Structure option creates a macro which can be run into the AVEVA base product to replicate the 
structure of the current project. No data is copied. When the user selects this option, a file browser is displayed 
so that the user can give the pathname for the macro. 
The application scans the System database and outputs to the file all the commands necessary to recreate the 
project structure, in the following order: 
• Create users. 
• Create teams. 
• Add users to teams. 
• Create Databases (DBs). 
• Make Copy DBs. 
• Create Multiple Databases (MDBs). 
• Add DBs to MDBs and make them current if appropriate. 
To create a new project using the generated macro, set up a virgin project using make as described in Create a 
Project. Then enter the AVEVA base product and run the macro by giving the command: 
$m/pathname/ReplicateProj.mac 
assuming that the user has used the default filename ReplicateProj when they created the macro. To give this 
command: 
• Either start the AVEVA base product in tty mode, (that is, without the Graphical User Interface (GUI)) by 
typing AVEVA E3D tty, and type the command into the window where AVEVA base product is running. 
or 
• Start the AVEVA base product with the GUI, select Display > Command Window, and type the command into

the Command window. 
Replicate On-premises Dabacon Project to Target Directory on Local LAN 
Supports the replication of an on-premises Dabacon Project to a target directory on a disk located on the local 
LAN. Replication to a target folder does not require the environment variables to be set prior to entering AVEVA 
Administration. 
To replicate the current project to an on-premises Dabacon Project on the local LAN, select Project > Replicate > 
Project data to a directory from the main menu to display the Replicate project to specified directory window. 
Browse 
Specifies the target folder for the replicated project. All project folders are downloaded into the selected folder. 
OK 
Replicates the current project. 
If the project is not locked, a confirm window displays, the user is prompted to lock the project. 
Open the Command Window to display details of the replication progress. Large projects can take some time to 
replicate. 
When the replication is complete, a summary displays in the Command Window. 
Command Syntax 
The command line syntax for project replication to a target directory is: 
REP/LICATE---directoryname
